original_abstract:
This article explores the intricate dynamic between human capital migration and the sustainable development of socio-economic systems. By examining the evolutionary role of migration, the study substantiates its inherently dual nature. On one hand, an influx of migrants can strain domestic social protection frameworks; yet conversely, leading developed nations—which serve as primary destinations—demonstrate high levels of socio-economic resilience while actively competing for high-quality human capital.

Through a comprehensive analysis of both the quantitative and qualitative characteristics of migration, this research identifies the primary drivers of internal and external population shifts within the EU and Ukraine. By tracking trend directions and the intensity of migration and emigration, the study constructs detailed socio-demographic portraits of migrants, contrasting profiles from the pre-war era with those emerging after the onset of full-scale conflict in Ukraine. Finally, the paper evaluates the balance of risks, losses, and opportunities inherent in these movements, identifying the resulting consequences for the achievement of Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) in both host and donor countries.
humanized_abstract:
The migration of human capital and the sustainability of socio-economic systems This paper analyses the complex relationship between the migration of human capital and the sustainability of socio-economic systems. Through the analysis of the evolution of migration and its dual character, this paper confirms on the one hand the negative impact of the influx of immigrants on the domestic social protection systems of the receiving countries, and on the other hand the high level of socio-economic stability in the main recipient countries which are at the same time competing with each other for the attraction of the best human capital.

The study performs an in-depth examination of the quantitative and qualitative aspects of migration, which reveals the main factors influencing internal and international migration of population within the EU and Ukraine. By tracing the dynamics of migration and emigration flows and assessing their trends and intensity, the study makes a detailed socio-demographic characterisation of migrants before and after the invasion in Ukraine. Based on the assessment of the balance of risks, losses and opportunities arising from international migration for sending and receiving countries, the study analyses the consequences of migration for realising the SDGs in these countries.
